*Description:*
Guildford Castle is in Guildford, Surrey, England. It is thought to have been built by William the Conqueror, or one of his barons, shortly after the 1066 invasion of England.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Abingdon Abbey
*Country:* england
*Region:* south east london
*County:* Oxfordshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 7th-16th century
*Coords:* 51.67055556, -1.27527778
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abingdon_Abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Abingdon%20Abbey/@51.6706,-1.2781,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Abingdon_Abbey%2C_geograph.jpg/330px-Abingdon_Abbey%2C_geograph.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/v2VEeRLPAT8mhjFMA
add source - https://www.abingdonabbeybuildings.co.uk/

*Description:*
Abingdon Abbey was a Benedictine monastery in Abingdon-on-Thames in the modern county of Oxfordshire in the United Kingdom. Situated near to the River Thames, it was founded in c. 675 AD and was dedicated to Mary, mother of Jesus. It was disestablished in 1538 during the dissolution of the monasteries. A few physical remnants of the Abbey buildings survive within Abingdon-on-Thames.

_Listing 2 of 270_

*Maps:* https://maps.app.goo.gl/fvt5gdozQpX34iLg8
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZOVRuvbdG-KZRWtVt6WsDmo3SFqFF2DcRkbvGDzmFWun0005AwwZ-mx1tYaqPE00nvxZdNwVfzcCwwCG6Ao3PQ6eSxJRcVhV6-5VrSIfvTlgjVXUW1RgHC7AuY2V_VvdnRkgvZ1YfZcwtEegw=s4800-w800
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://www.prinknashabbey.org/

*Description:*
Prinknash Abbey is a Roman Catholic monastery in the Vale of Gloucester in the Diocese of Clifton, near the village of Cranham, Gloucestershire. It belongs to the English Province of the Subiaco Cassinese Congregation, which is itself part of the worldwide Benedictine Confederation. It is noted for its manufacturing of incense.

_Listing 3 of 270_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Newark Castle (Nottinghamshire)
*Country:* england
*Region:* the midlands
*County:* Nottinghamshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 12th-17th century
*Coords:* 53.076298, -0.813549
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newark_Castle,_Nottinghamshire
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Newark%20Castle%20(Nottinghamshire)/@53.077,-0.812,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Newark_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6368033.jpg/330px-Newark_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6368033.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://newarkcastleandgardens.co.uk/

*Description:*
Newark Castle, in Newark-on-Trent in the English county of Nottinghamshire, was founded in the mid 12th century by Alexander, Bishop of Lincoln. Originally a timber castle, it was rebuilt in stone towards the end of the century. The castle was slighted (dismantled) in the 17th century as a result of the English Civil War, and restored in the 19th century, first by Anthony Salvin in the 1840s and then by the corporation of Newark who bought the site in 1889.

_Listing 5 of 270_

listing.name: Newark Castle (Nottinghamshire)
listing.desc: Newark Castle, in Newark-on-Trent in the English county of Nottinghamshire, was founded in the mid 12th century by Alexander, Bishop of Lincoln. The castle was slighted (dismantled) in the 17th century as a result of the English Civil War, and restored in the 19th century, first by Anthony Salvin in the 1840s and then by the corporation of Newark who bought the site in 1889.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Skenfrith Castle
*Country:* wales
*Region:* south wales
*County:* Monmouthshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 51.8784, -2.7902
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skenfrith_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Skenfrith%20Castle/@51.8545,-2.7834,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZPBxrt12N_ZAsJqljYoD9lCa20PRtgcfLkQUwV_55S7SYSkBoJMnHpzpwpe8IQ_4liuRafbe_-BFMLAgvUOVhr0JmSXcGUyXSo7csP_JMemhhysVzxJSEG3KllchatZN8FRh7JX9OT0sP6mQA=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Skenfrith Castle is a ruined castle in the village of Skenfrith in Monmouthshire, Wales. The fortification was established by the Normans in the wake of the invasion of England in 1066, to protect the route from Wales to Hereford. Possibly commissioned by William fitz Osbern, the Earl of Hereford, the castle comprised earthworks with timber defences. In 1135, a major Welsh revolt took place and in response King Stephen brought together Skenfrith Castle and its sister fortifications of Grosmont and White Castle to form a lordship known as the "Three Castles", which continued to play a role in defending the region from Welsh attack for several centuries.

_Listing 7 of 270_

listing.name: Skenfrith Castle
listing.desc: Skenfrith Castle is a ruined castle in the village of Skenfrith in Monmouthshire, Wales. The fortification was established by the Normans in the wake of the invasion of England in 1066, to protect the route from Wales to Hereford. Possibly commissioned by William fitz Osbern, the Earl of Hereford, the castle comprised earthworks with timber defences. In 1135, a major Welsh revolt took place and in response King Stephen brought together Skenfrith Castle and its sister fortifications of Grosmont and White Castle to form a lordship known as the "Three Castles", which continued to play a role in defending the region from Welsh attack for several centuries.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Aberystwyth Castle
*Country:* wales
*Region:* west wales
*County:* Ceredigion
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 52.41324, -4.08968
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aberystwyth_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Aberystwyth%20Castle/@52.4154,-4.0876,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Aberystwyth_castle_edit1.jpg/330px-Aberystwyth_castle_edit1.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Aberystwyth Castle is a Grade I listed Edwardian fortress located in Aberystwyth, Ceredigion, Mid Wales. It was built in response to the First Welsh War in the late 13th century, replacing an earlier fortress located a mile to the south. During a national uprising by Owain Glyndŵr, the Welsh captured the castle in 1404, but it was recaptured by the English four years later. From 1637 it housed a Royal mint of Charles I, which minted coins from locally mined silver. The castle was slighted by Oliver Cromwell in 1649.

_Listing 8 of 270_
